title: HIT OSLab Linux 0.11 Kernel
summary: A stripped-down Linux 0.11 kernel from Harbin Institute of Technology's OS lab course. Thirteen core subsystems — scheduler, process management, memory management, filesystem stack (buffer cache, superblock, bitmap, inode, namei, exec), signal handling, and two custom syscalls — have been stubbed out and must be fully implemented so the kernel compiles with gcc-3.4, boots under Bochs, and passes all automated tests.
instructionPreview: This is a Linux 0.11 kernel programming lab. The full linux-0.11 source tree is at `/workspace/oslab/linux-0.11/`. The kernel compiles with gcc-3.4 in C89 mode — do not use C99 features such as mid-block variable declarations. Multiple core subsystems have been stubbed out and you must implement them so the kernel compiles and boots correctly, and all tests pass.
instruction:
This is a Linux 0.11 kernel programming lab. The full linux-0.11 source tree is at
`/workspace/oslab/linux-0.11/`. The kernel compiles with gcc-3.4 in C89 mode — do
not use C99 features such as mid-block variable declarations. Multiple core
subsystems have been stubbed out and you must implement them so the kernel compiles
and boots correctly, and all tests pass.

Your working directory is `/workspace`. The project code is already present.
You need to implement all the modules and features listed below so that
all tests pass.

## Modules to Implement

The following modules need to be implemented or substantially built out:

- **Process Scheduler** (`kernel/sched.c`): The core task scheduler and related
  primitives. Includes the round-robin schedule loop, sleep/wakeup mechanisms
  for blocking I/O, timer management, and scheduler initialization that sets
  up the GDT/TSS/LDT entries and the system call interrupt gate.
  (~1 file, ~6 functions to implement)

- **Process Creation** (`kernel/fork.c`): The fork system call support. Includes
  address-space verification, memory segment copying for new processes, full
  process state duplication (registers, file descriptors, inodes), and PID
  allocation. (~1 file, ~4 functions to implement)

- **Signal Handling** (`kernel/signal.c`): Signal installation and delivery.
  Includes the signal() and sigaction() system calls for registering handlers,
  and the do_signal() dispatcher that sets up the user-space stack frame for
  signal handler invocation. (~1 file, ~3 functions to implement)

- **Process Exit** (`kernel/exit.c`): Process termination and waiting. Includes
  releasing task slots, sending signals to process groups (sys_kill), the
  do_exit() cleanup routine (freeing pages, closing files, notifying parent),
  and the sys_waitpid() system call. (~1 file, ~4 functions to implement)

- **System Calls** (`kernel/sys.c`): Miscellaneous system calls for process
  identity and permissions. Includes setreuid/setregid for changing user/group
  IDs with privilege checks, and setpgid for process group management.
  (~1 file, ~3 functions to implement)

- **Custom System Calls** (`kernel/who.c`): Two new system calls (iam/whoami)
  for storing and retrieving per-process name strings via user-space buffers.
  (~1 file, ~2 functions to implement)

- **Memory Management** (`mm/memory.c`): The virtual memory subsystem. Includes
  page table operations (freeing, copying, mapping), copy-on-write handling,
  page sharing between processes, and demand paging (page fault handler).
  (~1 file, ~7 functions to implement)

- **Filesystem — Block/Inode Allocation** (`fs/bitmap.c`): Free-space management
  for the Minix filesystem. Includes allocating and freeing both data blocks
  and inodes using bitmap manipulation. (~1 file, ~4 functions to implement)

- **Filesystem — Buffer Cache** (`fs/buffer.c`): The block device buffer cache.
  Includes buffer allocation (getblk), synchronous and read-ahead block I/O,
  page-sized block reads, and buffer pool initialization.
  (~1 file, ~5 functions to implement)

- **Filesystem — Inode Operations** (`fs/inode.c`): Inode management for the
  Minix filesystem. Includes the block mapping function (_bmap) that handles
  direct/indirect/double-indirect blocks, inode read/write to disk, inode
  allocation and deallocation (iput, iget, get_empty_inode, get_pipe_inode).
  (~1 file, ~7 functions to implement)

- **Filesystem — Path Resolution** (`fs/namei.c`): Pathname lookup and directory
  operations. Includes directory entry search and creation, path traversal,
  and the system calls for mknod, mkdir, rmdir, unlink, and link.
  (~1 file, ~14 functions to implement)

- **Filesystem — Superblock** (`fs/super.c`): Superblock management. Includes
  reading and caching superblocks, mount/umount system calls, and the
  mount_root() function that mounts the root filesystem at boot.
  (~1 file, ~6 functions to implement)

- **Filesystem — exec** (`fs/exec.c`): Program loading via execve(). Includes
  copying argument/environment strings between user and kernel space, setting
  up the LDT for a new process image, and the main do_execve() routine that
  reads the a.out binary header and sets up the new process.
  (~1 file, ~3 functions to implement)

## Test-Coupled Signatures

The following functions are called directly by the test suite. You must implement
them with these exact signatures:

- `int sys_iam(const char *name)` — store a user-space string (max 23 chars) into per-process kernel storage
- `int sys_whoami(char *name, unsigned int size)` — copy the stored name back to user-space buffer

The kernel has already been set up with syscall numbers (iam = 72, whoami = 73)
in `include/unistd.h`, declarations in `include/linux/sys.h`, `nr_system_calls`
updated to 74 in `kernel/system_call.s`, and `who.o` in `kernel/Makefile`.

The per-process storage array `proc_name[NR_TASKS][MAX_NAME_LEN + 1]` and the
constant `MAX_NAME_LEN 23` are already declared at the top of `kernel/who.c`.

The kernel must compile successfully with `make Image` in the linux-0.11 directory.

moduleDag.description: A stripped-down Linux 0.11 kernel from Harbin Institute of Technology's OS lab course. Thirteen core subsystems — scheduler, process management, memory management, filesystem stack (buffer cache, superblock, bitmap, inode, namei, exec), signal handling, and two custom syscalls — have been stubbed out and must be fully implemented so the kernel compiles with gcc-3.4, boots under Bochs, and passes all automated tests.

nodes.label: Virtual Memory Manager
nodes.description: Page-granularity virtual memory: page table copy/free, physical page reference counting, copy-on-write (un_wp_page/do_wp_page), page sharing (try_to_share), and demand paging (do_no_page loads missing pages from the executable). Fork and exec both depend on these primitives.

nodes.label: Process Scheduler
nodes.description: Round-robin task scheduler (schedule), blocking sleep/wakeup primitives (sleep_on, interruptible_sleep_on, wake_up), per-tick timer accounting (do_timer), and sched_init which sets up GDT/TSS/LDT entries and the int 0x80 system-call gate. All process-management modules depend on this.

nodes.label: Block Device Buffer Cache
nodes.description: Unified block-device buffer pool: getblk (LRU allocation/eviction), bread (synchronous read), bread_page (four-block read-ahead), buffer writeback, and buffer_init. Every filesystem module reads and writes disk blocks through this layer.

nodes.label: Signal Handling
nodes.description: Signal registration (sys_signal, sys_sigaction) and delivery (do_signal), which constructs the user-space stack frame so a registered handler runs on return from an interrupt or syscall, with a restorer trampoline to resume execution.

nodes.label: Custom System Calls (iam/whoami)
nodes.description: Two new syscalls wired into the dispatch table at slots 72/73. sys_iam stores a per-process name string (max 23 chars) from user space into a kernel array indexed by task slot. sys_whoami copies it back. Entirely self-contained; directly tested by the test suite.

nodes.label: Superblock and Mount Management
nodes.description: Reads and caches Minix superblocks: read_super loads the superblock and its inode/zone bitmap blocks from disk via the buffer cache; get_super is the lookup used by bitmap and inode layers. mount_root mounts the root device at boot. sys_mount/sys_umount handle runtime mount/umount.

nodes.label: Process Creation (fork)
nodes.description: Fork system call support: find_empty_process allocates a PID and task slot from the scheduler's task table; copy_mem assigns a new 64 MB LDT slot and duplicates page tables via mm/memory.c; copy_process clones all register state, open file descriptors, and inodes into the child task_struct.

nodes.label: Miscellaneous Process Syscalls
nodes.description: Process identity: sys_setreuid/sys_setregid change real/effective user and group IDs with privilege checks; sys_setpgid sets the process-group ID after locating the target task by scanning the scheduler's task array.

nodes.label: Filesystem Bitmap Allocator
nodes.description: Free-space management for the Minix filesystem: free_block/new_block manipulate the zone bitmap in the superblock's cached buffer to release and allocate disk blocks; free_inode/new_inode do the same for the inode bitmap.

nodes.label: Inode Management
nodes.description: In-memory inode cache and Minix block mapping: _bmap translates a logical block offset to a physical block number through direct/indirect/double- indirect zones, allocating new zones via fs_bitmap as needed. iget/iput manage reference counts and disk I/O. read_inode/write_inode sync to disk.

nodes.label: Process Exit and Wait
nodes.description: Process termination: do_exit calls free_page_tables to release address space, iput on pwd/root/executable inodes, closes all file descriptors, notifies the parent, and calls schedule() after marking the task ZOMBIE. sys_kill sends signals to process groups. sys_waitpid reaps zombie children.

nodes.label: Pathname Resolution and Directory Operations
nodes.description: Pathname-to-inode resolution (dir_namei, _namei, namei, open_namei) and directory-modifying syscalls: mknod, mkdir, rmdir, unlink, and link. Uses iget/iput from the inode layer and new_inode from the bitmap layer to create and remove directory entries.

nodes.label: Program Loader (execve)
nodes.description: do_execve: opens the binary via namei, reads the a.out header, copies argv/envp strings between user and kernel space, resizes the LDT for the new process image, zeros BSS, and sets up the initial stack pointer and instruction pointer for the new program.

edges.label: super.c calls bread/brelse/getblk to read the on-disk superblock and bitmap blocks
fork.c calls copy_page_tables, free_page_tables, and get_free_page to duplicate the parent address space
fork.c calls find_empty_process and accesses the task[] array and NR_TASKS managed by the scheduler
sys.c's setpgid scans the task[] array via find_task_by_pid, and setreuid/setregid manipulate current's credential fields
bitmap.c calls get_super to locate the zone and inode bitmap buffer blocks for a device
inode.c calls get_super to determine block layout when mapping logical blocks and syncing inodes to disk
inode.c's _bmap calls new_block/free_block to allocate/release data zones; iput calls free_inode when the last hard link is dropped
exit.c calls schedule() after setting the task to ZOMBIE state, and in the sys_waitpid wait loop
do_exit calls free_page_tables to release the text and data address-space segments
do_exit calls iput on current->pwd, current->root, and current->executable to release held inodes
namei.c calls iget/iput throughout path traversal and all directory-modifying system calls
namei.c calls new_inode (from bitmap.c) when creating new directory entries in mknod, mkdir, and link
do_execve calls namei() to resolve the executable path and obtain its inode
do_execve calls iput on the executable inode after mapping the program image
do_execve manipulates LDT base/limit and calls put_page/free_page to set up the new process image pages
